apple___alternaria_leaf_spot: Dark brown circular lesions with yellow halos appear because the Alternaria fungus releases toxins that destroy surrounding leaf cells, causing progressive necrosis and chlorophyll loss around the infection site.
apple___apple_scab: Olive-brown rough lesions develop because the scab pathogen colonizes the outer epidermal tissue, disrupting the normal leaf surface and reducing healthy photosynthetic activity.
apple___black_rot: Dark necrotic lesions with concentric rings form because the fungal pathogen expands outward from the primary infection point, producing repeated zones of tissue destruction.
apple___brown_spot: Multiple brown necrotic spots appear due to localized fungal growth that damages leaf cells and gradually weakens surrounding tissue.
apple___cedar_apple_rust: Bright orange rust-like lesions form because fungal reproductive spores develop inside the leaf tissue, creating visible pigment changes and raised infected areas.
apple___gray_spot: Gray necrotic patches surrounded by yellow tissue occur because infection destroys central cells while nearby chlorophyll breakdown causes surrounding discoloration.
apple___healthy: Uniform green leaf tissue with stable pigmentation, intact veins, and no visible cellular disruption indicates normal healthy physiological function.
banana___black_and_yellow_sigatoka: Black and yellow elongated streaks develop parallel to the veins because the Sigatoka fungus spreads through internal tissues and progressively destroys chlorophyll.
banana___cordana: Brown oval lesions with pale centers form because the pathogen kills central tissue first while the outer lesion margins continue expanding.
banana___healthy: Uniform green banana leaf tissue with smooth structure and normal venation indicates absence of visible disease.
banana___sigatoka: Dark elongated streaks spread along the leaf blade because fungal growth follows the internal structure of the leaf and damages photosynthetic cells.
cherry_including_sour___brown_spot: Brown necrotic lesions develop because fungal invasion causes localized collapse of leaf cells and gradually expands into surrounding healthy tissue.
cherry_including_sour___healthy: Smooth green leaf tissue with intact margins and no visible stress indicates healthy cherry leaf physiology.
cherry_including_sour___leaf_scorch: Burned brown leaf margins appear because impaired water transport causes dehydration and progressive tissue death from the edges inward.
cherry_including_sour___powdery_mildew: White powdery fungal growth forms because external fungal colonies spread across the surface and interfere with normal gas exchange.
cherry_including_sour___purple_leaf_spot: Purple bordered lesions form because fungal toxins damage cells while pigment accumulation occurs around the infected tissue.
cherry_including_sour___shot_hole: Small holes appear because infected tissue dies completely and later detaches from the leaf blade.
grape___black_rot: Black circular lesions with clearly defined borders appear because fungal colonization causes localized tissue death that expands gradually from the infection point.
grape___downey_mildew: Yellow oil-like lesions appear because the pathogen develops beneath the upper leaf surface and interferes with chlorophyll production in infected cells.
grape___esca_black_measles: Dark interveinal necrotic patches occur because vascular dysfunction reduces nutrient transport, leading to tissue collapse between the veins.
grape___healthy: Healthy grape tissue shows uniform green coloration, clear vein structure, and no visible stress symptoms.
grape___leaf_blight_isariopsis: Irregular brown lesions expand between veins because the pathogen spreads through weaker interveinal tissue and progressively kills surrounding cells.
grape___powdery_mildew: White powdery fungal growth develops because surface fungal colonies spread externally across the leaf and interfere with normal gas exchange.
guava___anthracnose: Dark sunken lesions develop because fungal penetration destroys internal tissues below the surface and creates expanding necrotic regions.
guava___healthy: Healthy guava leaves show balanced pigmentation, smooth texture, and no visible symptoms of infection.
guava___insect_bite: Irregular missing tissue appears because insects physically feed on the leaf surface and remove portions of healthy tissue.
mango___anthracnose: Dark sunken lesions develop because fungal infection penetrates below the leaf surface and causes deep internal tissue necrosis.
mango___bacterial_canker: Water-soaked lesions with yellow halos form because bacterial toxins damage cell walls and trigger chlorosis around infected tissue.
mango___die_back: Progressive drying from the leaf tip occurs because vascular blockage reduces water movement to outer tissues, causing gradual tissue death.
mango___gall_midge: Localized swelling appears because insect larvae stimulate abnormal cell division while feeding inside young tissue.
mango___healthy: Healthy mango leaves show smooth green tissue, normal venation, and no signs of structural damage.
mango___powdery_mildew: White powdery fungal growth forms because superficial fungal colonies spread over the leaf surface and reduce photosynthesis.
orange___citrus_canker: Raised corky lesions with yellow margins develop because bacterial infection causes cell rupture followed by defensive tissue thickening.
orange___foliage_damaged: General foliage damage appears as irregular tissue destruction caused by environmental stress, mechanical injury, or pest feeding.
orange___haunglongbing_citrus_greening: Yellow mottled blotches appear because the pathogen blocks phloem transport and causes uneven chlorophyll loss.
orange___healthy: Healthy citrus leaves display uniform green color and intact physiological structure.
orange___mealybugs: Leaf weakening and discoloration occur because sap-sucking insects remove nutrients and damage soft tissue.
papaya___anthracnose: Dark circular lesions form because fungal invasion destroys cells around the infection center and creates expanding necrotic zones.
papaya___bacterial_spot: Water-soaked irregular spots appear because bacterial infection breaks down leaf cells and causes surrounding yellow discoloration.
papaya___curl: Leaf curling occurs because disease stress disrupts normal cell expansion, producing distorted leaf growth.
papaya___healthy: Healthy papaya leaves show balanced pigmentation, normal structure, and no visible infection symptoms.
papaya___mite: Bronzing and roughening occur because mites feed on surface cells, causing epidermal damage and pigment loss.
papaya___mosaic: Patchy green-yellow discoloration appears because viral infection interferes with normal chlorophyll distribution across the leaf.
papaya___ringspot: Ring-shaped chlorotic patterns develop because viral movement creates circular zones of damaged tissue.
peach___bacterial_spot: Small water-soaked lesions darken over time because bacterial infection progressively destroys cells in the affected area.
peach___healthy: Healthy peach leaves show smooth surface texture and uniform green pigmentation without visible lesions.
strawberry___angular_leafspot: Angular lesions form because bacterial spread is restricted by leaf veins, creating sharply bounded infected areas.
strawberry___blossom_blight: Necrotic tissue develops because fungal infection damages delicate floral tissue and nearby leaf cells.
strawberry___healthy: Healthy strawberry leaves show natural green color, intact margins, and no visible stress.
strawberry___leaf_scorch: Reddish-brown margins appear because water loss causes progressive dehydration of outer leaf tissue.
strawberry___leaf_spot: Small circular lesions form because fungal infection kills localized clusters of cells.
strawberry___powdery_mildew: White powdery growth appears because fungal colonies expand externally and reduce normal leaf function.
